scenario: You enter a café and greet the waiter before asking for a seat.
cultureNote: In cafés, open with salam before asking for a seat.

scenario: You want to sit at a specific empty table.
cultureNote: Use aafak to soften the request and avoid sounding too direct.

scenario: You see a table and need to know whether it is free.
cultureNote: Ask before sitting if the table may be reserved or used by someone.

scenario: You need help choosing where to sit.
cultureNote: A simple question lets staff guide you without a long conversation.

scenario: You thank the waiter after being shown a seat.
english: Thank you, I will sit here.
cultureNote: A small thank-you keeps the café interaction warm.

dayTitleArabic: اختيار القهوة أو أتاي أو عصير
scenario: You want one Moroccan mint tea.
english: I would like one tea, please.
cultureNote: Atay is a familiar everyday café order in Morocco.

dayTitleArabic: اختيار القهوة أو أتاي أو عصير
scenario: You want a black coffee.
english: I would like a black coffee.
cultureNote: Short coffee orders are normal in cafés; no need for long wording.

dayTitleArabic: اختيار القهوة أو أتاي أو عصير
scenario: You want a Moroccan “nass nass”.
english: I would like a nass nass, please.
cultureNote: Nass nass is a common Moroccan coffee style: half coffee and half milk.

dayTitleArabic: اختيار القهوة أو أتاي أو عصير
scenario: You prefer orange juice.
cultureNote: Fresh orange juice is common in cafés and tourist areas.

dayTitleArabic: اختيار القهوة أو أتاي أو عصير
scenario: You ask what drinks are available.
cultureNote: This is a useful open question when you do not know the menu.

dayTitleEnglish: Sitting and ordering from the waiter
scenario: You need the menu before ordering.
cultureNote: French-origin words like menu are common in cafés.

dayTitleEnglish: Sitting and ordering from the waiter
scenario: You are ready to order.
cultureNote: Use this when the waiter is nearby but has not asked yet.

dayTitleEnglish: Sitting and ordering from the waiter
scenario: You ask what is available today.
cultureNote: A broad question works when the menu is not clear.

dayTitleEnglish: Sitting and ordering from the waiter
scenario: You point to an item on the menu.
cultureNote: Use hadi when pointing to something visible on a menu or display.

dayTitleEnglish: Sitting and ordering from the waiter
scenario: You confirm the order is complete.
cultureNote: Safi is a natural way to close an order.

scenario: Your order has not arrived yet.
friendlyLatin: smah liya, mazal ma ja talab
arabic: سمح ليا، مازال ما جا الطلب
english: Excuse me, the order has not come yet.
cultureNote: Start with smah liya to avoid sounding impatient.
pronunciationParts: smah liya / mazal ma ja / talab

scenario: You ask if the order will take long.
cultureNote: This is a polite way to ask about waiting time.

scenario: You accept waiting a little.
english: No problem, I will wait a little.
cultureNote: Showing patience is appreciated in busy cafés.

scenario: You need the order soon.
cultureNote: This is direct, so keep your tone friendly.

scenario: You thank the waiter for handling the delay.
cultureNote: A small thank-you can soften a delayed situation.

scenario: You ask for the bill.
cultureNote: Asking for l-hsab is a very common café sentence.

scenario: You ask where to pay.
cultureNote: Some cafés take payment at the table, others at the counter.

scenario: You ask if card payment is possible.
cultureNote: Small cafés may prefer cash, so ask before assuming.

scenario: You leave the change as a small tip.
cultureNote: A small tip is understood when service was good.

scenario: You end the interaction politely.
english: Thank you, have a blessed day.
cultureNote: Nhark mabrouk is warm and polite in daily service settings.

dayTitleEnglish: How are you and daily replies
scenario: You ask a friendly “how are you?”.
cultureNote: A short how-are-you can open social warmth.

dayTitleEnglish: How are you and daily replies
scenario: You reply that you are fine.
cultureNote: Hamdullah is a common natural part of positive replies.

dayTitleEnglish: How are you and daily replies
scenario: You ask the same back.
cultureNote: Asking back keeps the exchange balanced.

dayTitleEnglish: How are you and daily replies
scenario: You say everything is fine.
cultureNote: Short reassurance is useful when people ask about you.

dayTitleEnglish: How are you and daily replies
scenario: You thank the person warmly.
cultureNote: This phrase is a warm Moroccan way to thank or appreciate someone.

scenario: You open a café interaction politely.
cultureNote: Review: seat first, then order.

scenario: You order tea.
cultureNote: Review: one clear drink order.

scenario: You say you will wait.
cultureNote: Review: patience phrase.

scenario: You ask for the bill.
cultureNote: Review: close the café visit.

scenario: You end politely.
cultureNote: Review: end the moment clearly.

scenario: You start small talk about nice weather.
cultureNote: Weather is a safe low-risk small talk topic.

scenario: You comment that it is hot.
cultureNote: Heat is common small talk in Moroccan cities.

scenario: You comment that it is a bit cold.
cultureNote: Simple weather comments can start a friendly exchange.

scenario: You ask if it will rain.
cultureNote: Ask about rain when planning to walk or sit outside.

scenario: You ask for a shaded place.
english: I want a place in the shade.
cultureNote: Shade matters in hot weather cafés and terraces.

scenario: You ask the time.
cultureNote: Time questions are useful for meetups and waiting.

scenario: You say you will wait here.
cultureNote: This helps when meeting someone in a café.

scenario: You ask how long you should wait.
cultureNote: Useful when the waiter or a friend says “wait”.

scenario: You say you are in a bit of a hurry.
english: I am in a bit of a hurry.
cultureNote: Say it politely to avoid sounding rude.

scenario: You accept a delay.
english: If it is late, no problem.
cultureNote: A relaxed reply fits Moroccan café timing.

scenario: You ask if the person is from the city.
cultureNote: Ask gently; not everyone wants a long talk.

scenario: You explain that you are new here.
cultureNote: This explains why you may ask simple questions.

scenario: You compliment the café.
cultureNote: A compliment is a safe way to open light talk.

scenario: You say you want to speak a little Darija.
english: I want to speak a little Darija.
cultureNote: This invites patience from the listener.

scenario: You apologize for mistakes.
friendlyLatin: ila glt shi haja ghalta, smeh liya
arabic: إلا قلت شي حاجة غالطة، سمح ليا
english: If I say something wrong, excuse me.
cultureNote: A humble phrase makes practice feel friendly.
pronunciationParts: ila glt / shi haja ghalta / smeh liya

dayTitleEnglish: Asking for a chair or table
scenario: You ask if a chair is free.
cultureNote: Ask before taking a chair from another table.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking for a chair or table
scenario: You ask to take a chair.
cultureNote: This prevents awkwardness in crowded cafés.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking for a chair or table
scenario: You ask for a table for two.
english: We want a table for two.
cultureNote: Say the number clearly when arriving with someone.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking for a chair or table
scenario: You ask for a table by the window.
english: I want a table by the window.
cultureNote: Specific seats are fine to request politely.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking for a chair or table
scenario: You confirm the place is good.
cultureNote: A simple confirmation helps end the seating moment.

scenario: You want no sugar.
cultureNote: Sugar levels matter in Moroccan tea and coffee.

scenario: You want only a little sugar.
cultureNote: Shwiya helps you soften a preference.

scenario: You ask for a little more milk.
cultureNote: This is useful for coffee with milk or nass nass.

scenario: You do not want milk.
cultureNote: Negatives must be clear when ordering drinks.

scenario: You confirm the drink is good as it is.
cultureNote: Use haka when the drink is now correct.

dayTitleArabic: مقهى مزدحم أو لا يوجد مكان
scenario: You ask if there is an empty place.
cultureNote: Useful when the café is crowded.

dayTitleArabic: مقهى مزدحم أو لا يوجد مكان
scenario: You ask if the café is full.
cultureNote: M3mer means full or crowded in this context.

dayTitleArabic: مقهى مزدحم أو لا يوجد مكان
scenario: You ask if you can wait.
cultureNote: Waiting is normal in busy cafés.

dayTitleArabic: مقهى مزدحم أو لا يوجد مكان
scenario: You ask to be called when a table opens.
friendlyLatin: ila khwat shi table, aayet liya
arabic: إلا خوّات شي طابلة، عيط ليا
english: If a table becomes free, call me.
cultureNote: This is casual; use it with a friendly waiter.
pronunciationParts: ila khwat / shi table / aayet liya

dayTitleArabic: مقهى مزدحم أو لا يوجد مكان
scenario: You decide to come back later.
english: Okay, I will come back later.
cultureNote: A clean exit avoids pressure when the place is full.

scenario: You want to change your order.
english: I want to change the order, please.
cultureNote: Change quickly and politely before preparation finishes.

scenario: You no longer want that item.
cultureNote: This is direct; use a soft tone.

scenario: You switch from coffee to tea.
english: Make it tea instead of coffee.
cultureNote: Bla here means instead of or without depending on context.

scenario: You admit you ordered wrongly.
english: Sorry, I made a mistake in the order.
cultureNote: Taking responsibility makes the change smoother.

scenario: You thank them for understanding.
cultureNote: Use this when staff accepts a change.

scenario: You compliment the tea.
cultureNote: Food and drink compliments feel warm in Morocco.

scenario: You compliment the coffee.
cultureNote: Simple compliments can lead to friendly conversation.

scenario: You describe the place as calm.
cultureNote: Haniya carries a relaxed positive feeling.

scenario: You say the music is nice.
cultureNote: Small comments about the atmosphere are safe conversation starters.

scenario: You say you will return.
english: I will come back, God willing.
cultureNote: Inshaallah is natural for future intentions.

scenario: You say the city is beautiful.
cultureNote: A city compliment is an easy social bridge.

scenario: You ask about the old medina.
cultureNote: Old medinas are common visitor goals.

scenario: You ask if it is close.
cultureNote: Qriba is easier than distance details for beginners.

scenario: You ask what to see.
cultureNote: This invites a local recommendation.

scenario: You say you will go later.
english: I will go to the medina later.
cultureNote: This closes the city small talk clearly.

scenario: You say you are going to Fes tomorrow.
english: I am going to Fes tomorrow.
cultureNote: City names are easy anchors for travel talk.

scenario: You say you came from Casablanca.
cultureNote: Casa is commonly used for Casablanca in everyday speech.

scenario: You say you will stay two days.
english: I will stay here two days.
cultureNote: A short stay sentence helps daily conversations.

scenario: You say you want to see the country a little.
english: I want to see the country a little.
cultureNote: L-blad can mean the country or area depending on context.

scenario: You say traveling in Morocco is nice.
cultureNote: This expresses appreciation without a long explanation.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking about a nice nearby place
scenario: You ask for a nice nearby place.
english: Where is there a nice place nearby?
cultureNote: Ask locals for simple nearby recommendations.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking about a nice nearby place
scenario: You ask about a nearby garden.
cultureNote: French words like jardin are understood in many city contexts.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking about a nice nearby place
scenario: You ask where you can go now.
cultureNote: Useful when you have free time after a café stop.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking about a nice nearby place
scenario: You ask if there is a nice view.
english: Is there a nice view here?
cultureNote: Vue is commonly understood in tourism/café contexts.

dayTitleEnglish: Asking about a nice nearby place
scenario: You thank them and say you will see it.
english: Thank you, I will see it.
cultureNote: This closes a recommendation politely.

dayTitleEnglish: Handling speech you do not understand
scenario: You say you did not understand well.
cultureNote: Say this calmly instead of pretending to understand.

dayTitleEnglish: Handling speech you do not understand
scenario: You ask them to repeat.
cultureNote: Repeating is normal when learning Darija.

dayTitleEnglish: Handling speech you do not understand
scenario: You ask them to speak slowly.
cultureNote: Bshwiya is key for learner-friendly conversations.

dayTitleEnglish: Handling speech you do not understand
scenario: You explain you are still learning.
cultureNote: This makes people more patient with your Darija.

dayTitleEnglish: Handling speech you do not understand
scenario: You ask them to write it for you.
cultureNote: Writing can help when speech is too fast.

scenario: You joke that your Darija is still small.
cultureNote: A little self-humor makes practice easier.

scenario: You say you will try.
cultureNote: This keeps the conversation positive.

scenario: You say the person is helping a lot.
english: You are helping me a lot.
cultureNote: Appreciating help encourages more support.

scenario: You say the person made you laugh.
cultureNote: Use only in a friendly relaxed moment.

scenario: You compliment their way of speaking.
english: Your way of speaking is nice.
cultureNote: A light compliment should stay respectful.

scenario: You open small talk.
cultureNote: Review: daily greeting.

scenario: You comment on weather.
cultureNote: Review: safe icebreaker.

scenario: You ask for a nearby place.
english: Where is there a nice place?
cultureNote: Review: recommendation request.

scenario: You explain you are learning.
cultureNote: Review: learner identity.

scenario: You close politely.
cultureNote: Review: social closing.

scenario: Someone offers tea and you accept a little.
cultureNote: Accepting a small amount can feel polite in social settings.

scenario: You politely refuse for now.
cultureNote: Refuse warmly, not coldly.

scenario: You say another time.
cultureNote: This is a soft way to delay an invitation.

scenario: You accept but only for a short time.
cultureNote: Useful if you do not want to stay long.

scenario: You thank them for the invitation.
cultureNote: Thanking the invitation matters even if you decline.

scenario: You need to leave now.
cultureNote: Say it gently when ending a conversation.

scenario: You say it was nice meeting them.
cultureNote: A warm ending leaves a good impression.

scenario: You say you will see them later.
cultureNote: Good for people you may meet again nearby.

scenario: You say goodbye and wish a good day.
cultureNote: This is a clean polite close.

scenario: You thank them for the talk.
cultureNote: This reinforces the social purpose of the level.

dayTitleEnglish: At a café with a Moroccan friend
scenario: You ask where to sit together.
cultureNote: With friends, speech can be shorter and more relaxed.

dayTitleEnglish: At a café with a Moroccan friend
scenario: You ask what your friend drinks.
cultureNote: Ask before ordering together.

dayTitleEnglish: At a café with a Moroccan friend
scenario: You say what you will take.
english: I will take a nass nass.
cultureNote: Nass nass is a strong café identity phrase.

dayTitleEnglish: At a café with a Moroccan friend
scenario: You suggest splitting the bill.
cultureNote: Bill-splitting depends on context; ask lightly.

dayTitleEnglish: At a café with a Moroccan friend
scenario: You suggest leaving together.
cultureNote: Yallah is natural for moving to the next action.

scenario: You think the bill is a bit high.
english: Excuse me, the bill is a little high.
cultureNote: Be calm and respectful when questioning the bill.

scenario: You ask if everything is yours.
cultureNote: This clarifies if another table item was included.

scenario: You say you ordered only one item.
cultureNote: Use ghi wahed to clarify quantity.

scenario: You ask them to check the bill.
cultureNote: A question is softer than an accusation.

scenario: You say you understand now.
cultureNote: Close the misunderstanding politely.

scenario: You ask for help.
cultureNote: A direct help request is fine when polite.

scenario: The Wi-Fi is not working.
cultureNote: Useful in cafés where Wi-Fi is expected.

scenario: You ask if a charger exists.
english: I need a charger if there is one.
cultureNote: French tech words are common in Moroccan daily speech.

scenario: You ask for the bathroom.
cultureNote: This remains essential in cafés and restaurants.

scenario: You ask to call the waiter.
cultureNote: Use when you cannot reach the waiter yourself.

dayTitleEnglish: Explaining that you are learning Darija
scenario: You say you are learning Darija.
cultureNote: This sentence invites patience and support.

dayTitleEnglish: Explaining that you are learning Darija
scenario: You ask them to speak slowly.
cultureNote: Use this before practice becomes confusing.

dayTitleEnglish: Explaining that you are learning Darija
scenario: You ask them to correct you.
english: Correct me if I make a mistake.
cultureNote: This turns the social moment into learning.

dayTitleEnglish: Explaining that you are learning Darija
scenario: You say you want to speak like Moroccans a little.
english: I want to speak a little like Moroccans.
cultureNote: A friendly ambition phrase, not arrogant.

dayTitleEnglish: Explaining that you are learning Darija
scenario: You say Darija is hard but beautiful.
cultureNote: This often makes people smile and help.

scenario: You greet and ask how they are.
cultureNote: Start simple and confident.

scenario: You answer that you are fine.
cultureNote: A standard natural reply.

scenario: You say where you came from.
cultureNote: Use a placeholder so any learner can adapt it.

scenario: You use a safe weather comment.
cultureNote: Weather helps continue the conversation naturally.

scenario: You close the mini conversation.
cultureNote: End before the conversation becomes too difficult.

scenario: You find a seat.
cultureNote: Mixed review: café entry.

scenario: You order a drink.
english: I want a nass nass, please.
cultureNote: Mixed review: drink order.

scenario: You ask someone to speak slowly.
cultureNote: Mixed review: learner protection phrase.

scenario: You ask for a nice nearby place.
english: Where is there a nice nearby place?
cultureNote: Mixed review: recommendation.

scenario: You end politely.
cultureNote: Mixed review: social close.

scenario: You enter and ask for a seat.
cultureNote: Final simulation begins with seating.

scenario: You order your drink.
cultureNote: Final simulation: order with preference.

scenario: You make small talk.
cultureNote: Final simulation: friendly social comment.

scenario: You solve a small misunderstanding.
english: Sorry, I did not understand well.
cultureNote: Final simulation: protect yourself from misunderstanding.

scenario: You ask for the bill and leave.
cultureNote: Final simulation ends with bill and thanks.
